speaker: interviewer
text: And were in Los Angeles, California, March 20, 1995. Stanley, please continue. You were talking about going to the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and-- like-- you know, we get some supplies and some clothes for the people who worked with us to take back to Zakopane, which I did. I hired a Polack with a horse and a wagon. Whatever somebody brought, I took it with me. Coming back to the camp, I find out the next day they had the-- what you call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: A notice?

speaker: interviewee
text: A notice on the wall that they got three Jews-- they found on the-- on the railroad-- on the railroad car, and they all got shot because they left the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all three did leave the camp, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two were with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two were with you. But who was the third?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I guess the Polish-- the Polish guy, he didn't have papers. So he got killed, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think he was Polish? He wasn't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. We know that he was Polish, definitely. He was not with us. He just didn't have any-- any identification papers with him. There was nothing for the Germans, nothing to it. If you didn't have no papers, they can accuse you of being Jewish, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the German came on the train and he was looking for you and your friends, he was asking a particular name.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, he's asking, where is the [NON-ENGLISH]? In other words, [NON-ENGLISH] is a Jew. Like they say, where's the [NON-ENGLISH]? In other words, what I can figure out, somebody must have spilled or somebody must've told him that three Jews are on the train. That's all. Either Polacks saw-- saw me buying tickets, they went through. Or somebody, I don't know who, but it must've been somebody, must have told them-- told the German, I don't know if it was Gestapo or police-- he was in civilian clothes-- there were three Jews on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess a Pole died in your place.

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been a Pole died in my place. Because they look for the Jewish. They look for three Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And luckily, I tell you something, looking back, if the Jewish community didn't inquire about these people killed-- because if they would inquire that only two Jewish people are missing and one is still alive, they could've maybe got me, too, if they would inquire about them. You know what I mean? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I told them that only two Jewish people were on the train. They should do something about-- they should go to the Gestapo or something to find out what happened with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think the world learned from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it learned from the Holocaust? I don't know that-- that-- what they learned from the Holocaust. I hope they learned something from the Holocaust. Because if you see what's going on today, not that I am exactly a-- a good friend of the Serbs-- not the Serbs, the Croats. Because you know what the Croats and the Muslim Serbs did to the Jews, or to the Serbs, even-- not that [INAUDIBLE]. But what's going on over there today in Serbia, we see the pictures on TV with the concentration camps with the-- with the killing and-- and raping. People didn't know nothing about it. I don't think they-- they-- and-- they-- I don't think not only learn about it, they want to do something about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think the Jewish people learned anything from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, they did. Of course they did. We learned quite a bit. We gave everything-- we gave up our families, our-- our loved ones. And we learned that we cannot depend on anybody. That's why we have-- whatever we have, we have Israel. At least any place, let's say even today in-- in Sarajevo in Serbia, the Jews are air lifted to Israel. Something happens in Russia, the Jews are airlifted. They go. They have someplace where to go, and they fight for it. You know that, if the Jews could have fought during the war, if they had the means to defend themselves, they wouldn't have got killed so many Jews, maybe, at least they would have killed so many Germans, too. But you were teached at home not to fight back. The Jewish houses, before the war, especially in Poland, if you had some arguments with a Polack, or with-- or you came home you said some Polack beat you up, you said, get away. Don't start with him. Don't start with him again. Don't fight with him again. You know, let it get by. Let it get by. Jewish people just look-- look away, postpone it from day to day. They seen what's coming in Poland when it was very high anti-Semitism before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wouldn't let them in some universities. They wouldn't let them have certain trades, you know, in Poland. They were restricted. And they still-- they didn't have a choice. Where would they go? What could they do? What could they do? Is there anything else you'd like to add before we go to your pictures, before we look at your pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think I covered everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you did a very good job interviewing me. You got everything out of me, [LAUGHS] all that I had, what I had to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we still have to meet your family and look at your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would love to-- that you do that-- introduce my family and be on the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Stanley.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stanley, would you introduce you family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'd be glad to introduce you to my family. That's my wife, Fredka, our oldest son, Harvey, our son Jeffrey-- with his wife Linda. This is our youngest son, Michael. His wife didn't feel good today, so she's home. Her name is Sherry. This is our granddaughter, Rebecca. And that's Danielle. Jeffrey's little Claire-- a twin, had twins right away, the two girls. And Kyle-- Kyle is the son of Michael, and so is Shawn. And this is a little-- our new edition, the name is Stevie. And he's two months old. And he belongs to Linda and Jeffrey.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that's where he should go. Why don't you give him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could all the children tell us how old they are? Just call out how old everyone is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven and a half.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harvey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm 39.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Show them three. Show them three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: 35. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that having parents-- that your life was affected by the fact that you have parents who were Holocaust survivors? Who would like to answer that? Maybe each of you can respond to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it's a-- it's a-- it would be a difficult question for me, because I've only grown up with parents who were Holocaust survivors, so it's difficult to compare it to anything else. I think the thing that stands out to me, among other things, is that not only are my parents Holocaust survivors, but they were really one of the few survivors of their families. So we never had grandparents or uncles or cousins. We had second cousins, I think. Grandparents, uncles, aunts, or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I noticed other kids would visit their grandparents, or their uncles, or stuff like that. And we never did that. It's about the biggest thing. I'm sure there's a lot of others that my brothers can tell you about. Did it affect your social consciousness and the work that you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think we all have different ways of responding to events and circumstances. And I think my way of responding to the fact that my parents were Holocaust survivors, whether good or bad, was to try to stay away from that issue as much as I could. The more I learned about it, the more it bothered me. And therefore, the less I wanted to hear about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it that bothered you as far as talking about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe it's a personal thing. It just-- I've always been type of person, or at least I thought I was, who was, like, the protector on the schoolyard. If there was a younger or smaller kid getting picked on, I'd want to be there to help them. And I guess it's sort of like, when I was younger, I felt kind of guilty that I wasn't there to help out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work do you do, Harvey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm an attorney. But my specific work right now is working with a wonderful organization called Action. What Action does is it goes into schools and various other organizations and works with teens on all sorts of problems that they're having-- drug problems, problems relating to their parents, alcohol, violence, gangs. And they do that in various ways. And they work with groups of teens. And I think one of their biggest tools is-- is peer pressure, is they give the teams a place where it is cool or a good thing to be a good person. And they give them a chance to talk about the things that are really bothering them, away from a place where they're going to be judged. And in connection with that, they also work with the kids' parents, because the parents are a vital link in that. And they have feelings too. And they've gone through pain and trauma. And I am the, I guess you would call it, the governmental and corporate director, and director of relations for that entity. And it's been around for many, many years. And a wonderful organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeffrey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's an interesting question. I remember growing up my parents would tell us a lot of stories, some nice ones, some bad ones. But we were really told a lot about it, told about our family. And Harvey said, we didn't have many relatives. I was one of the few of my friends who didn't have grandparents and cousins and aunts and uncles. And while everybody was having family events, we, you know, didn't have that. And what was important, I remember growing up, that I would tell my friends about the stories growing up. And they had no idea about it. They had no knowledge of it, which was surprising to me, because we grew up around it. And we would go to temple and talk to friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought it was pretty universally known until you got around other people who-- a lot of them didn't always believe them. And you tell them the stories and it didn't seem to affect them the same way. Do you remember one of the stories you might have heard when you were young that you-- that made an impression on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were a lot that made an impression. But one I remember telling my wife Linda about, how hard it was-- we were seeing the movie And a lot of it hit home. And I remember my dad telling me a story that, and when he was released from the camp-- I don't-- if I'm remembering the story correctly, they didn't know if he was alive or not and they left him for dead. I think they threw him on with all the dead people and he was still alive. But he obviously survived out of it. He found some eggs, and broke them open and ate them. That was the only food he had. And his desire to survive was that important. And it really rang home when I was watching because a lot of people felt that situation, and I could finally identify with some other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: My turn?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would think of the three boys, that I was probably the most removed or sheltered, being that I was the youngest and had my older brothers as my influences, and as my-- as the ones I was trying to keep up with. So as far as trying to make sense of that, I probably didn't have as much of a time with it. Although after my bar mitzvah, my parents took me off to Israel. And we stopped off in Poland and toured the camps, which included Auschwitz. So they gave me quite a bit of a bird's eye view of the happenings, and became very aware of it. And prior to that and since then, it's been something that is, you know, very important to me as far as making sure that everybody that I know is aware, or at least that I do my part. And as you know, I'm a part of the Second Generation. So it's something that's very important to me as far as-- as keeping the thought of the Holocaust, you know, on my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can we take off on a break for a second?

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeffrey, your brothers had to leave. Do you have anything you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sure. We were talking about the past a lot. And it's very, very important in the history of everything. But also that people understand that something good came out of it. My parents came to America with basically nothing. My dad was in the war in Israel, which he's told us about. They came here with basically nothing and achieved a lot. Not only do you see the family around, and grandchildren, quite successful and very proud of their family, and very proud of their heritage. So I think it's important not only to dwell on the past but to see what came out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nicely put. Linda, would you like to add something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll add to what Jeff said, that his parents and their family are a very close-knit family. And they have a lot of love. And because they didn't have a lot of love, and they're just very giving, loving parents. And I'm glad to be a part of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it hard for you to sometimes understand the whole background and maybe the closeness of this family? I don't know anything about your background, so I don't know if it's similar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can understand and I can relate. And I know that, because they don't have a lot of family, I think it just makes them more special and more loving and more giving.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't want him to trip. Fredka, we're going to interview you in a few minutes, but I figure with your family right now, would you like to say something while your family is with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I want to say that I'm very happy that everything turned out the way it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone, anything else you'd like to add? Stanley?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to add that as you see here a wonderful, beautiful family, and our granddaughters, I'm so proud of them. They go to Hebrew school. They know all the questions for Passover. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Kyle goes, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kyle goes, too, Hebrew school [INAUDIBLE]. And they're good students. And Kyle is a very good baseball player right now. And they just start to play soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they know how to roller skate, right? And they-- and they-- they have the ballet lessons, they have ballet dances. And they make us very, very proud and very, very happy. Very thankful for everything we have. Do you want  to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Want to tell grandpa and grandma how much you love them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my grandma and grandpa a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's obvious. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. All these pictures survived by my family in Israel. There's the pictures from way before the war. Well-- we didn't have any-- we-- any pictures from the war did not survive because we didn't get any-- anything, there was nothing left. So anyway, those pictures are from my aunts and uncles, which were sent before the war to them to Israel. This is my mother. Her name was Mala Blumenstock. She married my father whose name was Gutreich. That picture was taken in the hometown of Nowy Targ. And I guess it must've been taken around in 1923 or '24.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think she was in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she must have been in the 20s. Must've been in the late 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she already married in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this is just before they got married. She-- she had-- she was a-- she was a good looking woman. She had-- a-- like a-- what do they call it-- not red hair, but chestnut, chestnut hair. Very good looking. This is a picture of my father. This picture was taken in his hometown, Nowy Sacz, which was not too far from my hometown, Nowy Targ. And it was taken before he got married-- I-- I presume it was 1922 or '23. And he was in the late 20s also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was-- his name was Chanine-- the Jewish name was Chanine. My mother used to call him Heinrich. Last name was Gutreich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Nowy Targ was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowy Sacz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nowy Sacz was in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. It's not too far from my hometown, Nowy Targ. It's a picture of my mother's family. It was taken-- as you see, I'm in the picture too, sitting right in the front in the white uniform, I must have been, here, about the eight years old. So that must have been taken in 1932. I would like to introduce the people in the picture from right to left, standing it the back. That's one of my uncles-- his came was Adolph, was a brother of my mother's. The second one was Michael, standing next to my mother. He was the youngest in the family, by the way. And the next is my mother, Mala. And at the end, this is my other uncle. His name was Siegfried. He was named Samuel, but he changed his name to Siegfried because he lived in Germany for a while. Sitting on the-- on the first row, from right to left-- that's this my grandmother, Mina-- Mina Blumenstock. Next to her is oldest daughter, whose name was Bronca. And next to her, sitting on the table, was my sister, Edith, which she was about five years younger than I was. And that's me in that white uniform. And on the very end is my grandfather-- no. Next to him is another aunt, Liba. She's a sister of my mother. And at the very end is my grandfather, Mayer Blumenstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, taken 1932. Because it looks to me like I was about eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else in the picture survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody survived but my uncle standing next to my mother, whose-- his name was Meckel. That's the one I just testified, my testimony that he used to live in Vienna. And he-- he was on the boat, on the boat going to Cuba, which was turned backed by the Cubans. And then he survive the war in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was the only one survivor. And after the war he came to New York-- to New York, and he settled in New York City. He passed away in 1972.

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is-- is-- is all the family from my father's side. As you see, it's the family of Gutreich. Sitting right in the center is my grandmother. My grandfather wouldn't take any pictures-- he was so pious that he would not take any pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you keep them there? Your grandchildren are in the background and I guess there's nothing we can do.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So let me continue. As I just said, my grandmother, on my father's side, that's-- that's grandmother sitting right in the center. That picture was taken about the 1934, '35. They gathered the whole family, as you see here. She had 12 kids, eight sons and four daughters. And they're all here in the picture. Sitting from left to right is my aunt Bronca. [INAUDIBLE] From left to right it is my aunt Bronca. She emigrated before the war to Israel. She went in the kibbutz. She just passed away last year. Next to her is my uncle, Ariel. He was the oldest in the family. He perished in the Holocaust. So did my grandmother, sitting next to her son. Now, you see a picture that we see holding up. This is my uncle and aunt living in Israel that year, in 1934. They send just a picture to complete the portrait of the family. That's one of their sons. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. Next to him is my uncle [NON-ENGLISH]. He didn't survive. He perished. Next to him is my aunt, Chana. She came from Berlin with her husband, which he's standing way behind. I'll tell you which one it is. She just came away from Berlin to Poland to be with the family. Next from left to right is the youngest son, whose name was Chaim. He was the orthodox. Then next to him is my aunt, Chaia. She left for Israel in 1936. She survived the war, as you know. She was in Israel. But she passed away about five years ago. Next to her is a brother named Joel, not Joel, Aaron. He is the husband of the lady sitting-- of my aunt-- sitting in the front row. They came from Berlin. Before the war, they emigrated to Israel and they survived. They passed away in Israel. Next to him is Aunt Sala. She-- she went to Israel and she survived in Israel. Next to her is an uncle named [NON-ENGLISH]. He perished in the war. Next to her is aunt, the youngest aunt, [NON-ENGLISH]. She survived the Holocaust and she is still in Israel. She's old lady living in Israel. Way back from left to right is my father. My father, Chanine. And next to him is uncle named Isaac. And that's the family Gutreich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's-- OK, that's enough. That's me. That picture was made right at the beginning of the war. That's an identification card. I found it right after the war in Poland. I must have been, here, about 15 years old. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was made in Nowy Targ, my hometown Nowy Targ. Let's get through the pictures. This is a picture done in my hometown in backyard of my house. And you see, I'm holding my brother. He was 10 years younger than myself. And there's my sister to-- to the right. I must have been, here, about 10 years old, 10 or 12 years old. That's it. [INTERPOSING VOICES] OK, that's a picture, a baby picture of myself. I must've been, here, about four years old. It was done in my hometown in Nowy Targ as a baby. That's a picture of my sister, done, I would say, in the 1930s. She must have been, here, about four years old. Done in the same hometown, in Nowy Targ. That's all the pictures that I have left from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Edith. Blonde-- blonde hair. Very good looking girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith. And last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name is Gutreich. Now there-- now that's the picture of my wife, Fredka. This is before we got married, done in Europe, in Munich, about the 1949. Her name was Fredka Eichen. And that's only one picture we have before we got married. That's the picture of my wife. And one to the right. OK, that's a picture of myself about a year after liberation. This was done in Germany. As you see, I have plenty of hair here, so it must have been done about eight or 12 months after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was done in Germany in the city we lived after the war. The name of the city is [NON-ENGLISH], It's in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is this 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning of '46.
